text: The laparoscope is introduced into the abdomen through the Palmer's point, providing an internal view of the abdominal cavity.
The laparoscopic view reveals the omentum minimally adhered to the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully separating the omentum from the anterior abdominal wall using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon identifies a small skin fistulization and proceeds to remove the previous proline suture that is causing a recurrent stitch abscess.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform adhesolysis, carefully separating the adhered omentum from the anterior abdominal wall.
An assistant applies pressure from above to reduce the hernia bulge, allowing the surgeon to access the contents of the hernia sac and ensure no omentum is left inside.
The surgeon inspects the hernia sac for any omental bubble trapped between the loops of the omentum.

The surgeon carefully separates the periphery of the adhesion, facilitating the removal of the omental content from the hernia sac. This ensures that all portions of the omentum trapped inside the sac are released, preventing any residual bulge in the defect area.
The surgeon pulls out the omentum from inside the hernia sac, revealing its contents.
The surgeon separates the remaining portion of the omentum from the hernia sac, ensuring that all omental tissue is released to prevent any residual bulge.
The surgeon pulls out some fat content from the hernia sac, identifying it as peritoneal fat rather than omental fat.
The surgeon identifies a proline knot that has caused a stitch abscess in the patient. The knot is visible and will be cut and removed.
The surgeon identifies a knot within the surgical field and prepares to pull it out using the harmonic instrument, leveraging gravity to assist in the maneuver.
The surgeon places a 15x15 cm mesh over the hernia site, preparing for trans-fascial fixation. The corners of the mesh are mapped on the skin to ensure accurate placement.

The surgeon marks a point approximately two centimeters lateral to the corners of the mesh. A small two-millimeter prick is made through the skin, allowing the use of a suture passer, varus needle, or cobbler's needle to secure the pre-tied vicryl corners of the mesh.
The surgeon fixes the lateral two corners of the mesh using trans-facial sutures, securing the mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon fixes the remaining two lateral sutures of the mesh, ensuring careful avoidance of the inferior epigastric vessels. The mapping of dangerous areas such as the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster is visually referenced to prevent complications.

The surgeon ensures the third corner of the mesh is stretched properly during ventral hernia repair, taking care to avoid the iliac and inferior epigastric vessels. The mesh is positioned two centimeters lateral to prevent corrugation or loose hanging.

The mesh remains stretched and properly positioned even after the removal of pneumoperitoneum gas, preventing shrinkage. The mesh is designed to integrate with the surrounding tissue, eventually becoming part of the rectus muscle as fibrous tissue forms between the mesh fibers.
A simple polypropylene mesh is displayed, showing its uncoated surface.

The surgeon uses an uncoated synthetic mesh, similar to that used in open surgery, to fix the fourth corner. The mesh is positioned to leave some rectus muscle between the suture pricks, ensuring the knot is tied subcutaneously while securely holding the rectus and muscle.

The surgeon tightens all corners of the mesh, ensuring it is completely stretched and symmetrically placed over the hernia site. The ProTac auto suture tacker is then used to fix the mesh at the remaining corners.
The surgeon maintains a two-centimeter distance between the mesh tacks for both the outer and inner crowning, ensuring the mesh is securely fixed in place.
The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament, which remains uncut. In cases of epigastric hernia, the falciform ligament is dissected before placing the mesh.

The surgeon ensures that the mesh projects at least six centimeters beyond the healthy margin of the hernia defect to prevent recurrence. The mesh is positioned to cover the defect even if it shrinks, and it may also pull the rectus in cases of small rectus diastasis. The inner crowning of the mesh is completed.

The surgeon prepares to conclude the hernia repair procedure, with plans to gradually evacuate the insufflation gas. The surgical setup includes a 10mm port at the palmar point and a 5mm port in the lumbar area on the left side.
